pattern: Every possible split point already has, on its left, the longest strictly increasing run ending there and, on its right, the longest strictly increasing run starting there -- so the best achievable k at that split is just the smaller of those two run lengths, and the overall answer is the best split.
hints: For every index, precompute how long a strictly increasing run is if it ends exactly there, scanning left to right.
Separately precompute how long a strictly increasing run is if it starts exactly there, scanning right to left.

For every possible boundary between two adjacent subarrays, the largest k usable at that boundary is the minimum of the ending-run length just before it and the starting-run length just after it; take the best boundary over the whole array.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, best split gives k = 3
statement example, best split gives k = 2
smallest possible array, two strictly increasing elements
smallest possible array, two equal elements
the whole array is one long strictly increasing run
every element is identical, no run longer than 1 exists anywhere
a long run on one side paired against a much shorter run on the other
strictly decreasing overall, every run has length 1

stress.description: large random arrays across the full value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 200000))
    nums = [rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: large arrays built from many short increasing runs, stressing the run-length computation
